vehicles.name: Notorious B.U.G.
vehicles.blurb: A 2014 Club Car Precedent that looks far more dependable than it is. Beloved, temperamental, and piloted by a four-year-old with no regard for his own safety or the boathouse railings.

photos.alt: Green four-seat Club Car golf cart parked beside a tidal cove at sunset, lobster and fish decals on the side
photos.caption: Golden hour at the boathouse, looking far more dependable than it is.

vehicles.name: The Appletree Falcon
vehicles.home: My parents' place (the former Appletree Inn)
vehicles.blurb: A 2003 Club Car DS that has quietly outlasted the inn it's named for. Steadier than the B.U.G., though that is a famously low bar.

vehicles.name: The Red Dragon
quirks: Starts on the first kick. Every time.
vehicles.blurb: A 1991 Honda CT70 with roughly 2,000 miles on it that starts on the first kick, every single time. It does not break down. It exists, in part, to make the carts feel bad about themselves. The benchmark.

photo.alt: A hand holds the removed starter solenoid above the B.U.G.'s open, dusty engine bay; yellow rubber boots stand on the grass nearby
photo.caption: The culprit, held aloft like a trophy. Six weeks of downtime in one greasy little cylinder.

photo.alt: The green B.U.G. golf cart parked on a waterfront lawn at dusk, beside a calm cove with Adirondack chairs on a dock and the Maine shoreline beyond
photo.caption: Operational again after the F/R switch swap — pictured pretending it never refused to reverse.

troubleshooting.title: No-Start Troubleshooting
troubleshooting.subtitle: 2014 Club Car Precedent · gas · Kawasaki FE350 · 12V key-start, starter-generator + solenoid
troubleshooting.intro: Two jobs happen when you turn the key and press the pedal: a high-current path (battery → solenoid → starter-generator) that spins the engine, and a low-current trigger circuit that tells the solenoid when to connect it. This guide chases the trigger circuit.
safety: It will move. Several tests run with the cart in gear. Chock the wheels front and back, set the brake, and stay out of its path.
Bridging arcs. Touching a screwdriver or jumper across battery-level posts throws sparks and can spot-weld. Brief taps only, eye protection, remove rings and watches.
Never upsize or bypass the fuse. If the 10A fuse keeps blowing, you have a short — find it. A 15A fuse, or a wire where the fuse goes, is how a salt-corroded harness becomes a fire.
Hot exhaust. The muffler is brutal right after running. Let it cool before reaching near it.

triage.title: Step 1 — Split the problem (the big-post test)
triage.body: In neutral, brake set, wheels chocked, bridge the two large solenoid posts with a screwdriver.

outcomes.result: It cranks (and runs if you feed gas)
outcomes.meaning: Battery, cables, starter-generator, engine, and main grounds are all good. Your fault is in the trigger circuit — walk the steps below.
The problem is on the high-current side: dead/low battery, a bad main cable or ground, the starter-generator, or the solenoid's main contacts. Chase those instead.

triage.note: On the B.U.G. it cranks — so the rest of this guide is the trigger circuit.

steps.label: Key switch
steps.title: Key switch (blue output wire)

meter.test: Probe the blue wire where it leaves the switch.
meter.bad: 0V — dead/corroded key switch, or no feed reaching it (check the hot side; it should read ~12V in either key position).

noMeter.test: Jumper the switch's hot feed terminal to the blue output terminal (forces it ON).
noMeter.good: If dead things downstream now come alive, the key switch was your open. Keep a real 10A fuse in, do it briefly, and remove the jumper after — the key is also your shutoff.

steps.first: Unplug the red/blue/green connector behind the switch and check the pins for green/white crust. Salt corrosion here mimics a dead switch perfectly. Clean, grease, reseat.

steps.label: Forward/Reverse switch
steps.title: Forward/Reverse switch (the in-gear interlock)
steps.intro: A 3-terminal micro switch on the shifter; it must close (cart in F or R) for the circuit to continue. This was the failed part on the B.U.G.

meter.test: Probe its output with the shifter in F.
meter.good: ~12V in gear, 0V in neutral (that's the lockout working).
meter.bad: Stuck at 0V even in gear — corroded/dead F/R switch.

noMeter.test: Bridge the switch's terminals (or drop in a known-good spare and hot-wire it).
noMeter.good: If it now cranks in gear, the F/R switch is bad.

part.name: Club Car 3-terminal forward/reverse micro switch
part.note: The 2-prong cousin is 1014808. Get the 3-prong so all original wires land and any second function (reverse buzzer) still works. Cheap — buy a multi-pack and keep spares.

steps.label: Accelerator (GCOR)
steps.title: Accelerator GCOR switch (at the gas pedal)
steps.intro: The start switch is built into the pedal assembly up front, with a 6-pin sealed Delphi connector: orange/green = start (N.O.), black/white = kill (N.C.).

meter.test: Check ~12V on the feed side, and continuity across the orange/green contact when you press the pedal (or trip it by hand).
meter.bad: Voltage but no continuity on press = dead switch or the linkage isn't tripping it.

noMeter.test: At the harness plug, bridge orange → green (fakes the pedal pressed), key ON, in F/R, wheels chocked, pedal untouched.
noMeter.good: Cranks → the GCOR switch is bad. Nothing → the fault is upstream. Bridge only orange-to-green; pull the jumper the instant it fires.

steps.first: Unplug the Delphi connector, inspect both halves for corrosion, tug each wire to catch a backed-out pin, clean, dielectric grease, reseat until it locks.

steps.label: Solenoid
steps.title: Solenoid (coil + ground)
steps.intro: If 12V reaches the solenoid's small trigger post but it still won't crank, the solenoid or its ground is the issue.

meter.test: Coil reads a few ohms across the two small posts; its ground terminal hits chassis under ~1Ω.
meter.good: A few ohms across the coil; ground under ~1Ω.

noMeter.test: In neutral/chocked, jump 12V across the two small posts (battery+ to one, ground to the other).
noMeter.good: Clicks AND cranks = solenoid good. Click but no crank = bad contacts. No click = dead coil — replace the solenoid.

steps.note: Polarity: if the solenoid has an internal diode across the coil, the small wires are polarity-sensitive — reversed, the diode shorts and blows the fuse. Ohm the coil one way, flip the leads: same reading = no diode; very different = diode present, match factory colors (blue and orange on their original posts).

outcome.label: Cranks
outcome.note: All line up (key ON, in F or R, pedal pressed) → solenoid coil energizes, solenoid snaps closed, battery power hits the starter-generator, and it cranks.

noMeterLadder.title: Find the dead link without a meter (the jumper ladder)
items: Jump 12V straight to the solenoid's small orange (trigger) post. Cranks → everything from the solenoid down is fine; the open is upstream. No crank → it's the solenoid/coil/ground.
Working back up, repeat the bridge tests at the GCOR and the F/R switch. The first point where bridging makes it crank tells you the bad component is just upstream of there.

fuse.title: If the 10A fuse keeps blowing
fuse.intro: A fuse that blows repeatedly is a short to ground, not a tired switch — and a blown fuse kills the whole trigger circuit, so it masks every step above. Clear the short first.
items: Clue from timing: blows the instant the key is ON = short on the always-hot side. Blows only when you press to start = short in the start branch. Random / on bumps = a chafed wire grounding out with vibration (the classic salt-air failure).
With a test light: clip it across the empty fuse slot. It glows while the short is live. Wiggle and disconnect branches one at a time until it goes dark — that's your shorted branch.

No tools: branch isolation. Fresh 10A fuse, disconnect one branch (e.g. the solenoid's small trigger wire), key ON. If the fuse now survives, the short is in that branch; if it still pops, it's upstream. Repeat until isolated.
Then: find the worn spot — run your fingers along the harness for bare copper, especially where wires cross metal edges or brackets near recent work. Repair and insulate.
fuse.safety: Again: never run a bigger fuse or a wire in the fuse slot to get around this. That's the fire.

wireLegend.meaning: the GCOR start contacts (normally-open, close when you press the pedal)
the GCOR kill contacts (normally-closed; ignore for a no-crank)
battery positive, on a large solenoid post
the other large solenoid post, out to the starter-generator

weakPoints: Forward/Reverse micro switch (OEM 1014807) — confirmed failure here.
Corroded grounds — solenoid coil ground and the engine-to-frame strap. Clean to bare metal.
GCOR pedal connector — sealed Delphi plug at the pedal; corrosion or a backed-out pin.
Chafed control wiring — an intermittent short that blows the 10A fuse.
Key switch / its connector — internal corrosion or crusty pins behind it.
Solenoid — coil, contacts, or (if equipped) a polarity-sensitive diode.

lines: Big-post bridge test cranked — battery, cables, starter-generator, engine all good.
Replaced the starter solenoid and a 10A fuse; did not fix it.
Found and cleaned the GCOR pedal connector; did not fix it.
The 10A fuse was blowing intermittently (a separate corroded-harness short).
Root cause: a dead Forward/Reverse micro switch, confirmed by hot-wiring a known-good spare.
Fix: replace with OEM #1014807 (3-prong). Keep spares on the shelf.
